s1_b1: A spinning flywheel, going nowhere. Its center never moves.
s1_b2: By the usual rule, motion energy comes from a thing's speed through space. This thing's center has none.
s1_b3: So does a spinning wheel carry energy of motion — or not?
s1_b4: Try to stop it with your hand. It bites back. It clearly carries something.
s1_b5: Look closer. The center is still — but every other bit of the disk is racing in a circle.
s1_b6: Each tiny piece has its own motion energy. Add them all up, and the wheel as a whole holds plenty.
s1_b7: That total is rotational kinetic energy. Energy of spin, not of going anywhere.
s1_b8: And where the mass sits matters. The same mass, far from the axis, moves faster — and stores far more.
s1_b9: That reluctance to spin up or slow down has a name of its own — the moment of inertia.
s1_b10: It's why a flywheel can store energy, and a potter's wheel keeps turning long after the last push.
s1_b11: A rolling ball carries both kinds at once — moving forward and spinning. Two energies in one body.
s1_b12: Computing rotational kinetic energy from the moment of inertia and the angular speed — that's yours.
